body_txt: So you're a writer, and you want to instantly clue your audience in to the fact that a character in your story is a genius or at least wise. One possible way is to give them glasses . However, there is another, easy-to-recognize visual shorthand that has the same effect, especially if the character is associated with a university or other academic setting: have them wear a mortarboard. The mortarboard, also known as the square academic cap (the name derives from the fact that its appearance is similar to the mortarboard used by brickmasons to hold mortar), is a head dress that is closely associated with academic institutions, especially in the Anglo-Saxon world and the United States. Along with the academic dress , it used to be regular attire at universities and colleges (and still is in older institutions like Oxford) for the faculty, in particular the senior teachers. This tradition continued well into the first half of the twentieth century: as the headdress and accompanying full-length gown covered the whole body, it stopped professors from getting blackboard dust on their smartest clothes. Although it has largely fallen out of use in real life (except as part of graduation ceremonies), the mortarboard nevertheless remains ingrained in the public consciousness as a sign of higher education and a visual shorthand for a teacher/professor, and thus associated with intelligence — even in countries where it was never used, if only through Pop Culture Osmosis . Characters that follow this trope tend to wear a mortarboard all the time, not just during a graduation ceremony. Sometimes, these characters go even further and have them wear a full graduation robe. It is also often part of the attire for The Owl-Knowing One . Sub-Trope of Hollywood Dress Code . This trope is nowadays almost exclusive to stories aimed at children, however. Adults know that in Real Life , mortarboards and graduation robes are almost never worn outside of graduation ceremonies at educational institutions. But it is a quick shortcut for kids to see intelligence. For a hat that conveys the opposite effect, see Dunce Cap .
